In this lesson, we're going to be talking about understanding the front end sale. So what is the front end? Well, there are really two categories of marketing in any direct response business and it's critical that you understand the difference between these two categories and the purpose of these two categories. The first category is what we call the front end and the front end of your business is all of the marketing that you do with prospects to convert them into a new customer. In other words, this is all of the marketing that you're doing with individuals that have yet to purchase a product or service from you. And the goal of the front end, of course, is to turn those prospects into new raving customers. The second category of marketing in a direct response business is what we call the back end. Now, the back end is all of the marketing that you do with customers, with individuals that have already purchased at least one or more products or services from you. And the objective with the back end is to deepen your relationship with those customers and generate additional transactions. There's a big difference between the front end and the back end, not only in the way you market, but more importantly in the way you view the success of a front end promotion and a back end promotion. Now, we're going to be talking about that later, the difference really between the two, but for now, I really just want to drive home the overall main purpose of each. So for the front end, your goal is to generate maximum new customers. What I mean by that and why I'm sharing that, it seems kind of obvious, but is that the goal of the front end, the goal of all of the marketing that you do with prospects, with individuals that have yet to become customers, the goal is to generate maximum new customers. It's not to generate profit. The mistake that a lot of novice and new marketers make is that they don't view a difference between the front end and the back end. They think that the entire purpose of both the front end and the back end is to generate a profit from every single transaction. This is why the typical small business owner doesn't have in their mind two categories of marketing. To them, marketing is marketing. Marketing to a prospect is all about generating a profit. Marketing to a customer is all about generating a profit. Well, the way you grow a business fast is by viewing the front end simply as a vehicle to generate maximum new customers, not as a profit generation tool. All of the profit in your business comes from the back end. The back end, all of the marketing that you do with existing customers is where all of the profit is in your business. The main purpose of the back end is to grow what we call average lifetime customer value. Average lifetime customer value is really nothing more than the average amount of money that the average customer spends with you over the life of their patronage with your business. It's basically the average amount of money that a customer will spend with your company. Your goal on the back end is to offer more and more products and or services to customers so that you can grow the average dollar amount that the typical customer is worth your business. Now, let me tell you how the A players view each. I already really alluded to this. This is critical because you'll see that this view is very different from the way the typical marketer, the typical small business owner, understands their front end, their back end, or really just understands their marketing in general. A players view the front end simply as a vehicle to acquire customers. As I already said, not a source of profit. Your goal with the front end is if you invest hypothetically $100 in marketing or in advertising, your goal is to generate $100 worth of new customers. It's not to generate a profit. The A players then view the back end, all of the offers and products and services that are presented to existing customers as the real business that we're in and where all of the profit comes from. See, I want you to understand that the money in your business is on the back end. The most expensive activity in any online business is the acquisition of customers. That is the front end. Our goal with the front end is to basically pay for your advertising, to break even so that if you spend $100 or $500 or $1,000, if you invest any amount of money in advertising on the front end, our goal is to recapture that money while we acquire new customers. The profit then comes from the back end offers because all of the costs associated with acquiring a new customer was taken care of on the front end. There are three main objectives of your front end offer. We're going to be talking about your front end offer in a module coming up shortly where we talk about what's called the customer captivation offer. This is your main entry point offer that you're going to learn all about. The front end sale, the front end offer, the offer that we're making to prospects has three objectives. First and foremost, it's to attract the right type of customer. We're going to talk a little bit more about this later, but when I say the right type of customer, it is a quality customer, a customer that has a deep interest in the topic, the content, the solutions that you provide. The right type of customer is a customer that has the highest likelihood of investing more and more money with you over time, again, the back end. The second objective, of course, of your front end offer is to convert a prospect into a new customer. That is pretty self-explanatory. The goal is to turn prospects into new customers. The third objective, which is the objective that a lot of novice marketers don't understand, is that the purpose of your front end sale, the purpose of putting a new product into the hands of a brand new customer is to set up the sale of your first back end offer. In other words, is to create desire, create interest, create curiosity for the next product that you are going to offer to this new customer. You're going to learn how to do this in just a little bit, but these are the three main objectives of the front end sale.
